hero.title: Rules for Sorting, Rules for Living
hero.subtitle: How Waste Sorting Presumes the 'Ideal Citizen'
hero.body: Waste sorting is not treated here as a neutral instruction. The exhibition asks why a practical task can produce moral discomfort, and how local rules construct a normal user who is expected to recognise, obey, and take responsibility at the point of disposal.

meta.label: Team
meta.value: Lunyu CHEN, Xinyi HUANG, Zhuoying LAO, Xinyi LONG, Sze Wa TING, Xiaoqi XIE

hero.team: Lunyu CHEN, Xinyi HUANG, Zhuoying LAO, Xinyi LONG, Sze Wa TING, Xiaoqi XIE

venue.title: JCCAC sets the scale for a public encounter.
venue.body: The proposal is planned for L0 Gallery at Jockey Club Creative Arts Centre, a public-facing cultural site in Shek Kip Mei. The venue matters because the exhibition speaks to residents, students, migrants, and everyday users who meet waste policy through routine disposal.
facts: L0 Gallery, Jockey Club Creative Arts Centre
30 Pak Tin Street, Shek Kip Mei, Kowloon
venue.imageAlt: Exterior view of Jockey Club Creative Arts Centre in Hong Kong.
venue.caption: Jockey Club Creative Arts Centre, the proposed venue context for the L0 Gallery exhibition.
venue.source: Image source: WiNG, Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 3.0.

premise.title: A routine civic act becomes a test of the self.
premise.body: The project begins from the position of the new resident: a reset position before local sorting norms have become common sense. From there, the rule can become questionable again. The exhibition moves through three linked claims: exposure, mechanism, and consequence.

cards.label: Exposure
cards.title: Contradictory Standards
cards.copy: The same object is governed differently across local systems, yet each rule appears as the answer.

cards.label: Mechanism
cards.title: Moralised Compliance
cards.copy: Compliance turns from an administrative requirement into a moral obligation, producing anxiety, shame, and judgement.

cards.label: Consequence
cards.title: Displaced Responsibility
cards.copy: Responsibility is shifted onto individuals for consequences shaped by production, packaging, infrastructure, and regulation.

comparison.title: The same item. Different rules.
comparison.body: Recycling rules are local, but everyday items are not. The comparison below turns the visual logic of the proposal into readable page text: the same object is reorganised by different systems, materials, cleaning conditions, and policy assumptions.

rows.note: Food and oil contamination prevents paper recycling, so the object is routed toward residual or general waste.
Cartons sit between paper packaging and food or beverage packaging, with cleaning rules varying by site.
Bags are treated as plastic in sorting, but policy controls differ between recycling streams, charging systems, and single-use rules.
The same cup can be treated through material, contamination, lining, market regulation, or disposal limits.

comparison.sources: Comparison content adapted from the provided Wastend proposal slide and policy notes.

participation.title: Instruction is delayed so uncertainty can become visible.

steps.title: Guess before the Rules
steps.detail: Visitors classify familiar waste objects before any official categories are revealed. Decision-making comes before explanation.

steps.title: Reveal
steps.detail: Wall panels show how the same object is classified across Hong Kong, Japan, South Korea, Denmark, and Sweden.
prompts: Each rule appears correct inside its own system

steps.title: Reflect and Archive
steps.detail: The Archive Wall turns private hesitation into a shared trace and treats confusion as curatorial evidence.
prompts: The rule that confuses me most is...
What I most want to ask is...
If I could change one rule, I would...

floorPlan.title: The gallery distributes the argument.
floorPlan.imageAlt: Floor plan for Rules for Sorting, Rules for Living at L0 Gallery.
floorPlan.caption: L0 Gallery sequence: entry, Archive Wall, documentary clip, moralised compliance works, then the displaced responsibility section.

sections.title: Section 1: Contradictory Standards
sections.copy: Near the entrance, visitors first experience uncertainty through the participatory sorting activity. Their choices then move onto the Archive Wall before being reframed through a documentary interview on foreign residents learning Japanese disposal rules.

sections.title: Section 2: Moralised Compliance
sections.copy: Barbara Kruger's works frame sorting as an experience of being watched, judged, and measured. Casa Grande offers a counter-model, asking whether public language can invite rather than discipline.

sections.title: Section 3: Displaced Responsibility
sections.copy: The final section expands the frame beyond the bin, moving from global plastic circulation to labour, value, production, and packaging design.

floorPlan.source: Image source: provided curatorial plan document.

artworks.title: The works move the question beyond the bin.

items.title: Untitled (Questions), 1991; Untitled (We Are the Objects of Your Suave Entrapments), 1984
items.alt: Two Barbara Kruger works using photographic images and declarative text.
items.role: Frames a practical rule as silent scrutiny: being seen, measured, and positioned by public language.
details: Kruger's image-text strategy turns public address into a field of pressure.
In this exhibition, the works help visitors read waste instructions as social judgement, not only neutral information.

items.title: Friends of the Orphan Signs - Casa Grande, 2016
items.alt: Roadside signs from Friends of the Orphan Signs - Casa Grande.
items.role: Offers a counter-model to disciplinary public language, showing that signs can also affirm experience and agency.
details: The project repurposes abandoned public signs with new statements.
Placed after Kruger, it asks whether a rule or sign can support people rather than simply correct them.

items.title: Washed Up, 2010-present
items.alt: Green plastic bottles arranged among rocks by the shoreline.
items.role: Connects local sorting habits to global plastic circulation and the industrial responsibility hidden behind consumer disposal.
details: Plastic debris becomes a visible record of global movement and accumulation.
The work moves the exhibition from individual disposal decisions to wider systems of production and circulation.

items.title: The Quality of Mercy, 2012
items.alt: A suspended sculptural installation made from post-consumer plastic bottles and lighting.
items.role: Reminds visitors that waste does not end at the correct bin; it continues to exist in other forms and systems.
details: Robson's plastic forms make discarded material physically present and difficult to ignore.
The installation supports the exhibition's move from sorting obedience to material afterlife.

items.title: Waste Land / Pictures of Garbage, 2008
items.alt: Large-scale portraits made from garbage and photographed from above.
items.role: Reintroduces human dignity and labour into the chain of waste, shifting attention from objects to people who handle them.
details: Muniz's project connects waste material with the workers and communities who process it.
It expands the question of responsibility beyond the person standing at a recycling bin.

items.title: The Plastic Bag Store, 2020
items.alt: An immersive supermarket installation with objects made from single-use plastic.
items.role: Traces waste back to production and packaging design, asking why difficult-to-recycle products are made in the first place.
details: The work imitates retail display while revealing the absurd volume of single-use packaging.
It closes the exhibition by shifting attention from correct disposal to product design and consumer infrastructure.

outcomes.title: Not better obedience, but a more critical relation to rules.
items: Recognise anxiety and shame as systemic effects rather than personal failure.
See local sorting rules as arrangements, not natural truths.
Turn private hesitation into public reflection through participation and archive.
Shift attention from obedience to structure, governance, and responsibility.

resources.title: Only the supports that shape the public programme remain visible.
resources.heading: Required supports
supports: GREEN @ COMMUNITY program for policy interpretation and educational resources
Home Affairs Department for contact channels with local community centres and immigrant groups
The Hong Kong Federation of Youth Groups for sponsorship, volunteer teams, and local partnerships
